option: Distinctive Serif Display + Neutral Sans Body (e.g. a characterful serif for H1/H2, a clean grotesk for body/UI)
best_for: Editorial, portfolio, and premium brand sites wanting a headline moment that doesn't read as default SaaS sans-everywhere
avoid_when: Data-dense dashboards where a display serif at small sizes hurts legibility — reserve the serif for genuinely large headline sizes only
tradeoffs: The serif carries all the personality/distinctiveness while the sans keeps body text maximally legible — this split does real work instead of using one 'safe' font everywhere

option: Monospace Accents Within a Sans-Serif System (labels, tags, code, metadata in mono; everything else in a humanist sans)
best_for: Technical/developer products wanting precision cues without going full monospace-everywhere
avoid_when: Consumer-facing marketing pages where monospace accents can read as 'trying too hard to look technical'
tradeoffs: A small, consistent monospace touch (timestamps, tags, version numbers) signals technical precision cheaply, without the readability cost of monospace body text

option: Single Variable Font, Weight/Width Doing All the Differentiation Work
best_for: Products wanting maximum consistency and smaller font-loading payload — one variable font file replaces what would otherwise be 2-4 separate font files
avoid_when: A brand wanting a genuine visual contrast between display and body type — a single typeface family, however varied its weights, has an inherent ceiling on differentiation compared to true pairing
tradeoffs: Modern variable fonts (weight, width, sometimes optical size axes) can produce a full type system from one file, improving performance (QA045/QA049) while still allowing real hierarchy through weight/width variation

option: Skip the Default System/Framework Font Unless Deliberately Choosing It
best_for: Any brand-forward surface (marketing site, portfolio) — Inter/system-ui/the framework's bundled default is the single most common tell of an unstyled or templated-feeling site
avoid_when: A genuinely utility-first internal tool where font choice truly doesn't matter to the product's goals — even then, verify this is a deliberate call, not an oversight
tradeoffs: Inter and system-ui are excellent, legitimate typefaces — the issue isn't quality, it's that using them with zero customization is the most statistically common choice, so it reads as 'didn't make a type decision' rather than 'made this decision'

option: Choose a Typeface With at Least One Distinctive Letterform, Not Purely Neutral
best_for: Wanting a type choice that's recognizable/memorable at a glance (a distinctive 'g', 'a', or numeral style)
avoid_when: Extremely dense UI text at small sizes where a distinctive letterform could hurt legibility — reserve distinctive faces for headline/display sizes specifically
tradeoffs: A typeface that's technically 'neutral' (like many default UI sans fonts) is chosen specifically to disappear — a brand-forward surface benefits from at least one typeface with genuine character that a viewer could recognize again

option: Modular Type Scale With an Unusual Ratio (not the default 1.25/1.333), e.g. 1.5 (perfect fifth) for more dramatic size jumps
best_for: Brands wanting headline sizes to feel genuinely large/confident rather than incrementally bigger than body text
avoid_when: Dense information UI where a dramatic scale would consume too much vertical space across many heading levels
tradeoffs: Most default type scales use the same 1.2-1.333 ratio range — a deliberately larger or smaller ratio produces a visibly different rhythm that's a cheap, systemic distinctiveness lever applied consistently across every heading level

option: Tighter-Than-Default Line Height on Large Display Text (1.0-1.1 for headlines, 1.5-1.6 preserved for body)
best_for: Headline/hero text specifically — default line-height values (often 1.5 universally) look loose and un-designed at very large sizes
avoid_when: Body text and any paragraph content — QA-standard line-height (1.5+) stays important there for readability regardless of what headlines do
tradeoffs: Large display text with generous line-height looks like body-text styles were never adjusted for scale — tightening specifically at headline sizes (while keeping body text spacious) is a small, high-visual-impact fix

option: Selective Small Caps or Letter-Spaced Uppercase for Labels/Eyebrows Only
best_for: Section labels, category tags, and 'eyebrow' text above headlines — a small, consistent typographic treatment that signals structure
avoid_when: Body copy or long-form text — letter-spaced uppercase hurts readability at paragraph length and should never be applied there
tradeoffs: A consistent micro-treatment (e.g. 11px, 0.08em letter-spacing, uppercase) used only for structural labels creates a recognizable rhythm throughout a page without touching primary readability

option: Italic or Alternate Style for Emphasis, Not Bold-Everywhere
best_for: Editorial and long-form content wanting varied emphasis without every emphasized word shouting via bold weight
avoid_when: UI copy/microcopy where italic can reduce legibility at small sizes — bold remains the right tool for UI emphasis specifically
tradeoffs: A body copy system using italics for genuine emphasis (rather than bold for everything) reads as more considered and editorial, reserving bold's higher visual weight for cases that actually need it

option: Tabular Figures for Any Numbers That Align Vertically (tables, prices, stats)
best_for: Data tables, pricing displays, dashboards — proportional (default) figures have varying widths that cause numbers to visually misalign in columns
avoid_when: Inline numbers within prose paragraphs, where proportional figures actually read more naturally than tabular ones
tradeoffs: A specific, easy-to-miss detail: most fonts default to proportional figures, causing columns of numbers to jitter left/right — enabling tabular figures (a font-feature-settings/OpenType feature) fixes this with zero layout changes needed

option: Preload Only the Above-the-Fold Critical Font Weight(s)
best_for: Every production site — this is BE/QA047's font-loading guidance applied at the type-system-design stage: decide up front which weights are truly critical-path
avoid_when: A font system already reduced to a single variable font file (UX023) — there's only one file to consider preloading there, simplifying this decision
tradeoffs: Overusing font-display and preload on every weight variant is nearly as bad as under-optimizing — decide deliberately which 1-2 weights are above-the-fold-critical and let the rest load normally

option: Next.js — next/font for Automatic Self-Hosting and Zero Layout Shift
best_for: Next.js projects — next/font (Google Fonts or local) self-hosts and automatically applies size-adjust metrics to prevent the layout shift a swapped web font would otherwise cause
avoid_when: A project not on Next.js — the equivalent concern still applies (self-host fonts, use font-display: swap, match fallback font metrics) but via different tooling per framework
tradeoffs: Next.js's font system solves QA042's CLS concern specifically for web fonts almost automatically, which is easy to get wrong by hand (matching fallback font metrics manually is fiddly)

option: Serif Numerals/Display Digits Paired With a Sans Body for a Premium-Editorial Feel
best_for: Financial, luxury, or editorial products wanting numbers themselves (prices, stats) to carry a premium visual signal, not just headline text
avoid_when: Data-dense tables needing maximum numeral legibility at small sizes — save distinctive numeral styles for hero/featured numbers, not table cells
tradeoffs: Most type pairing advice focuses on headline vs. body — treating a hero number/statistic with its own distinctive numeral style is a less common, higher-impact-per-effort distinctiveness technique

option: Variable Grotesk Single-Family (one variable font, weight axis 300-900 carrying all hierarchy, e.g. Inter Variable or custom)
best_for: Product UI and design systems wanting one-font simplicity with real hierarchy range and small payload
avoid_when: Brand-forward marketing pages needing a distinct headline voice — a single grotesk everywhere is the definition of the default look
tradeoffs: One variable file beats loading 4 static weights on performance; hierarchy discipline must come entirely from the weight/size scale since there's no second family to signal register shifts

option: High-Contrast Didone Display + Humanist Sans Body (fashion-magazine serif for hero sizes only, warm sans for everything else)
best_for: Fashion, beauty, luxury e-commerce, and editorial brands wanting immediate print-magazine authority
avoid_when: Didones collapse at small sizes — hairline strokes vanish below ~28px, so any use in UI chrome or body text fails legibility
tradeoffs: The strongest instant-luxury signal in typography; strictly display-only usage discipline required, with the humanist body doing all functional work

option: Monospace Display + Sans Body (code-voice mono for headlines/labels, neutral sans for body)
best_for: Developer tools, AI/data products, and technical brands wanting the terminal aesthetic without full-mono fatigue
avoid_when: Non-technical audiences where mono reads cold or gimmicky; also fails as body text at length — mono paragraphs tire readers fast
tradeoffs: Instantly signals technical credibility; mono at display sizes needs tightened letter-spacing since default mono tracking reads gappy when large

option: Rounded Sans Display + Neutral Sans Body (soft-terminal rounded face for headlines, straight-edge sans for UI)
best_for: Education, kids/family products, health, and friendly consumer brands wanting warmth without a script font
avoid_when: Enterprise/authority contexts where rounded terminals read juvenile; discipline needed to keep rounded out of dense UI controls
tradeoffs: Approachability with far more polish than defaulting to a single rounded font everywhere; the straight-edged body keeps forms and tables serious

option: Condensed Grotesk Display + Wide-Stance Body (tight condensed caps for headlines, normal-width sans with open spacing below)
best_for: Sports, automotive, events, and news brands wanting compression energy and headline density
avoid_when: Calm/luxury contexts — condensed caps shout by design; also fails where headlines are long sentences rather than short punches
tradeoffs: Width contrast between display and body creates hierarchy without any color/weight help; condensed faces need generous line-height or stacked headlines collide

option: Slab Serif Display + Geometric Sans Body (sturdy slab for headlines, clean geometric for body/UI)
best_for: B2B products wanting confidence and groundedness without corporate-serif stuffiness or grotesk sameness
avoid_when: Delicate/elegant brand positioning — slabs are heavy by nature and read industrial-sturdy, not refined
tradeoffs: Slabs are underused in SaaS (distinctive by scarcity) and hold weight at small display sizes better than didones; pair discipline matters since two geometric personalities can clash

option: Serif Body + Sans UI Split (readable text serif for long-form content, sans for all interface chrome)
best_for: Docs, publications, and knowledge products where people read for minutes at a time
avoid_when: Short-burst UI-heavy products — if there's no sustained reading, the serif never earns its load cost
tradeoffs: Serif body text measurably aids long-form reading comfort and instantly separates content from chrome; requires a genuinely screen-hinted text serif, not a print face at 16px
